questions.questionText: The most common type of surgical wound classification that has an infection rate of <2% is:
questions.optionB: Clean wound (Class I — no break in sterile technique, no viscus entered)
questions.explanation: Surgical wound classification: Clean (Class I): no break in technique, no viscus entered, infection rate <2%. Clean-contaminated (Class II): controlled entry into GI/respiratory/GU tract, rate <10%. Contaminated (Class III): gross spillage, open fresh wounds, rate ~20%. Dirty (Class IV): established infection, perforated viscus, rate ~40%. Reference: Bailey & Love — Wound Classification.

questions.questionText: Informed consent for surgery must include:
questions.optionB: Nature of procedure, risks, benefits, alternatives, and consequences of refusal — obtained voluntarily from a competent patient
questions.explanation: Valid informed consent: (1) Patient competent (age ≥18, sound mind), (2) Voluntary, (3) Informed — nature of operation, expected benefits, material risks, alternatives, consequences of refusal. Written consent for surgical/invasive procedures. Emergency: implied consent (life-saving). Minors: parent/guardian consent. Reference: Bailey & Love — Consent.

questions.questionText: ASA (American Society of Anesthesiologists) physical status classification III indicates:
questions.optionB: Patient with severe systemic disease that limits activity but is NOT incapacitating
questions.explanation: ASA classification: I — normal healthy. II — mild systemic disease (e.g., well-controlled HTN, mild DM). III — severe systemic disease, functionally limiting but not incapacitating (e.g., poorly controlled DM, COPD, morbid obesity, stable angina). IV — severe incapacitating disease, constant threat to life (e.g., unstable angina, CHF, renal failure). V — moribund, not expected to survive 24h. VI — brain-dead organ donor. 'E' suffix: emergency procedure. Reference: Bailey & Love — ASA.

questions.questionText: The most appropriate fluid for initial resuscitation in hemorrhagic shock is:
questions.optionB: Isotonic crystalloid (Ringer's lactate or Normal saline — 3:1 rule for blood loss replacement)
questions.explanation: Hemorrhagic shock: initial resuscitation with warm isotonic crystalloid (RL preferred — more physiologic, or NS). 3:1 rule: 3mL crystalloid for every 1mL estimated blood loss. Class I (<15% loss): crystalloid. Class II (15-30%): crystalloid. Class III (30-40%): crystalloid + blood. Class IV (>40%): massive transfusion protocol (1:1:1 PRBC:FFP:platelets). Signs: tachycardia (earliest), hypotension (late — lost >30%). Reference: ATLS — Hemorrhagic Shock.

questions.questionText: The WHO Surgical Safety Checklist has three phases:
questions.optionB: Sign In (before anesthesia), Time Out (before incision), Sign Out (before patient leaves OR)
questions.explanation: WHO Surgical Safety Checklist (2009): reduces surgical complications and mortality. Three phases: (1) SIGN IN: before induction — confirm identity, site, procedure, consent, allergies, airway risk, blood loss risk. (2) TIME OUT: before skin incision — team introduction, confirm patient/procedure/site, anticipated critical events, antibiotic prophylaxis given, imaging displayed. (3) SIGN OUT: before patient leaves OR — instrument/sponge/needle counts, specimen labeling, equipment issues, key recovery concerns. Reference: WHO — Safe Surgery.
